name: Mitra
description: Mitra, a prominent deity in the Rigveda, is a significant figure in early Hindu mythology, often associated with Varuna. He embodies the concept of contracts, friendship, honesty, and agreements between people. Together, Mitra and Varuna are often invoked as a dual divinity, representing the cosmic order and moral law. Mitra is seen as the upholder of truth and righteousness, ensuring that promises and oaths are kept. He is the embodiment of the light of dawn and the rising sun, contrasting with Varuna's association with the night and cosmic waters. While Varuna is associated with the external cosmic order (Rta), Mitra governs the internal moral order and interpersonal relationships. He is considered a benevolent and friendly god, fostering harmony and cooperation among humans. Some scholars believe that Mitra's origins might be traced back to Proto-Indo-Iranian religious beliefs, where he held a similar role as a god of contracts and covenants. His influence, however, gradually diminished in later Hindu scriptures, with other deities like Vishnu and Shiva gaining prominence. Nevertheless, Mitra remains a vital part of the Vedic pantheon, representing the importance of trust, loyalty, and the sanctity of agreements.
subtitle: God of contracts, friendship, and honesty.

keyMyths.title: Mitra and Varuna: The Dual Divinity
keyMyths.description: Mitra and Varuna are often invoked together, representing the cosmic and moral order. Varuna oversees cosmic law, while Mitra governs human relationships and contracts.

keyMyths.title: Mitra as the Upholder of Truth
keyMyths.description: Mitra is responsible for ensuring that promises and oaths are kept, punishing those who break their word. He embodies the importance of honesty and integrity.

keyMyths.title: Mitra and the Rising Sun
keyMyths.description: Mitra is associated with the light of dawn and the rising sun, symbolizing new beginnings and the renewal of contracts and friendships. He represents the benevolent aspect of the sun.

sources.author: Ralph T.H. Griffith
